Title: Script Service & Collaborative Editor
Description: Build the Script Service (AST persistence, version snapshots, project/script lifecycle) and the real-time collaborative screenplay editor powered by Loro CRDT. This epic is the first to deliver user-visible value: writers can create scripts, edit them in professional Final Draft-quality formatting, and collaborate in real-time with convergent CRDT sync. Sprint 2 delivers the Script Service and project/script navigation UI. Sprint 3 delivers the CRDT Collaboration Service and the TipTap-based screenplay editor with the Loro ProseMirror binding. The Loro POC gate at Sprint 3 Week 1 is the highest-risk decision point in the roadmap — if Loro fails two or more criteria the team falls back to the Yjs strategy documented in Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md. The dogfood milestone (Week 8) is achieved when the engineering team writes real scripts in ScriptOS daily. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprints 2 and 3, Wiki/03-data-model.md, Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Writers can create projects, add scripts, and navigate the script library in the web app
- Script AST schema (19 node types) persisted in PostgreSQL via Atlas migrations with all indexes and constraints
- Script Service gRPC methods (upsertASTNodes, getSceneSnapshot, createVersion, lockDraft, unlockDraft) all passing integration tests
- script.checkpointed NATS JetStream event published after every checkpoint; downstream consumers notified
- CRDT POC gate: Loro passes all 5 criteria documented in Wiki/04; go/no-go decision recorded in wiki before implementation proceeds
- Two writers co-editing the same script see converged content within 200ms under normal network conditions
- Scene move, split, and merge operations produce consistent results across all connected clients
- Checkpoint cycle writes ast_nodes rows readable via getSceneSnapshot gRPC within 5s of checkpoint trigger
- Dogfood milestone (Week 8): engineering team writes scripts in ScriptOS daily
- All Docker images built and deployed to staging; all Kubernetes health checks passing
Tags: script-service; collaboration; crdt; editor; sprint-2; sprint-3

Title: Script Service Core
Description: The Script Service is the authoritative data store for all screenplay content in ScriptOS. It owns the script.* PostgreSQL schema — projects, scripts, ast_nodes, and script_versions tables — managed via Atlas declarative migrations. It exposes a GraphQL subgraph for client queries and mutations, and a gRPC API for inter-service calls (used by the Collaboration Service for checkpoints and by the Temporal publish-saga for locking). It publishes the script.checkpointed NATS domain event that triggers downstream re-processing (Breakdown, Search). This is the first Tier 1 service deployed after Auth and every subsequent service depends on it. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 2, Wiki/03-data-model.md PostgreSQL DDL, Wiki/16-system-design.md Script Service.
Acceptance Criteria:
- createProject and projects queries functional via Apollo Router
- createScript and script(id) query working end-to-end
- upsertASTNodes gRPC batch insert/update succeeds for up to 500 nodes in a single call and completes in under 500ms
- getSceneSnapshot gRPC returns heading, element counts, and page estimate for a scene
- createVersion stores CRDT binary snapshot in script_versions; updates scripts.current_version_id
- lockDraft and unlockDraft gRPC methods guard the publish saga lifecycle with transactional lock checks
- script.checkpointed NATS JetStream event published with scriptId, orgId, changedSceneIds, versionId, timestamp
- Script Service Docker image deployed to staging; /health returns 200
- Atlas migration for script.* schema applied cleanly to staging Postgres
Tags: script-service; backend; postgres; graphql; grpc; sprint-2
Parent: Script Service & Collaborative Editor

Title: As a writer I can create script projects and episodes and browse my script library from the web app
Description: Writers need to organise their work into projects (a feature film or a TV series) and scripts within a project (individual episodes). The Script Service must provide CRUD operations that back the project/script navigation UI. Projects are scoped to an organisation; all mutations must enforce org isolation via the X-Org-ID header injected by Apollo Router. Archived projects must not appear in the default list. The GraphQL subgraph must be federated into Apollo Router so the frontend can query via the unified gateway. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 2 backend tasks — project CRUD, script CRUD, GraphQL subgraph.
Parent: Script Service Core

Title: Create Atlas schema for script.* PostgreSQL tables with all indexes and constraints
Description: Define the declarative Atlas schema for the script service PostgreSQL tables in packages/db/schema/script.hcl. Tables to create: (1) script.projects — id uuid default gen_random_uuid() PK, org_id uuid NOT NULL, title text NOT NULL, script_type text NOT NULL CHECK (IN 'feature_film','tv_pilot','tv_episode','short','web_series'), status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active', archived_at timestamptz, created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz DEFAULT now(). (2) script.scripts — id uuid PK, project_id uuid NOT NULL FK→script.projects ON DELETE CASCADE, title text NOT NULL, script_type text, status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft' CHECK (IN 'draft','locked','archived'), saga_run_id text, current_version_id uuid, created_by uuid NOT NULL, created_at, updated_at. (3) script.ast_nodes — id uuid PK, script_id uuid NOT NULL FK→script.scripts, parent_id uuid, node_type text NOT NULL, position integer, metadata jsonb DEFAULT '{}', revision_color text, created_at, updated_at. (4) script.script_versions — id uuid PK, script_id uuid NOT NULL FK, version_label text, crdt_snapshot bytea NOT NULL, created_by uuid, created_at DEFAULT now(). Indexes: idx_projects_org on (org_id, archived_at), idx_scripts_project on (project_id), idx_ast_nodes_script on (script_id, parent_id), idx_ast_nodes_type on (script_id, node_type). Run atlas schema apply and confirm all tables and indexes exist in staging Postgres.
Parent: As a writer I can create script projects and episodes and browse my script library from the web app

Title: Implement project CRUD gRPC methods and GraphQL mutations in the Script Service
Description: Implement project-level operations in services/script/src/handlers/project.handler.ts. gRPC: CreateProject(CreateProjectRequest{orgId, title, scriptType, createdBy}) returns CreateProjectResponse{id}; ListProjects(ListProjectsRequest{orgId, includeArchived, pageToken, pageSize}) returns ListProjectsResponse{projects[], nextPageToken}; ArchiveProject(ArchiveProjectRequest{id, orgId}) — sets archived_at = now(). GraphQL resolvers in services/script/src/graphql/project.resolver.ts: createProject mutation calls CreateProject gRPC; projects query calls ListProjects with cursor pagination; archiveProject mutation. Use raw pg with typed queries — no ORM (for predictable query plans). Each mutating operation writes an audit entry: INSERT INTO audit.log (actor_id, org_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, timestamp). Integration test: create org → create project → listProjects asserts length 1 → archiveProject → listProjects asserts length 0 → listProjects(includeArchived: true) asserts length 1.
Parent: As a writer I can create script projects and episodes and browse my script library from the web app

Title: Implement script CRUD gRPC methods and GraphQL mutations in the Script Service
Description: Implement script-level operations in services/script/src/handlers/script.handler.ts. gRPC: CreateScript(CreateScriptRequest{projectId, title, scriptType, createdBy}) returns CreateScriptResponse{id}; GetScript(GetScriptRequest{id}) returns ScriptResponse{id, projectId, title, status, currentVersionId, sceneCount, createdAt, updatedAt}; ListScripts(ListScriptsRequest{projectId}) returns ListScriptsResponse{scripts[]}; UpdateScript(UpdateScriptRequest{id, title?, status?}) returns ScriptResponse. sceneCount computed as SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ast_nodes WHERE script_id = id AND node_type = 'scene'. GraphQL resolvers: createScript mutation → gRPC; script(id) query → GetScript; scripts(projectId) query → ListScripts. Validate projectId belongs to the caller's org before creating script. Integration test: createProject → createScript → scripts(projectId) returns 1 result → script(id) returns correct metadata including sceneCount = 0.
Parent: As a writer I can create script projects and episodes and browse my script library from the web app

Title: Add Script Service GraphQL subgraph to Apollo Router supergraph with Federation v2 directives
Description: Register the Script Service GraphQL subgraph in Apollo Federation v2. (1) In services/script/src/graphql/schema.graphql add @key directives: type Project @key(fields: id) and type Script @key(fields: id). Add User reference resolver: extend type User @key(fields: id) { id: ID! @external; projects: [Project!]! }. (2) Add Script Service to apps/gateway/supergraph.yaml: name: script, routing_url: http://script-service:4002/graphql. (3) Run rover supergraph compose to regenerate the supergraph schema file. (4) Update Apollo Router Kubernetes ConfigMap with the new supergraph schema; trigger rolling restart. (5) Integration test via Apollo Router: query { script(id: testId) { id title status } } returns correct data. (6) Verify Federation entity resolution: query User.projects via the federated gateway returns the user's project list. Document the subgraph boundary in Wiki/16-system-design.md.
Parent: As a writer I can create script projects and episodes and browse my script library from the web app

Title: As a service consumer the Script Service provides gRPC APIs for AST node management version snapshots and draft lifecycle control
Description: The Collaboration Service writes checkpointed AST state to the Script Service via gRPC. The Temporal publish-saga locks and unlocks script drafts via gRPC. The Script Service must be a reliable gRPC server with well-typed proto definitions, proper error codes, and idempotent operations. The upsertASTNodes method is the critical path for CRDT checkpointing — it must handle batches of up to 500 nodes and complete in under 500ms. The lockDraft method must use a pessimistic row-level lock to prevent race conditions with concurrent saga runs. Reference: Wiki/03-data-model.md Script AST Node Types, Wiki/07-workflow-orchestration.md Publish Saga, packages/proto/script.proto.
Acceptance Criteria:
- upsertASTNodes(scriptId, nodes[]) batch upserts up to 500 ASTNode rows using INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE; completes in under 500ms for a 100-node batch in staging
- getSceneSnapshot(sceneId) returns { sceneId, headingText, sceneNumber, elementCounts: {action, dialogue, character}, estimatedPageCount, status }
- createVersion(scriptId, snapshotBytes, versionLabel, createdBy) inserts into script_versions; updates scripts.current_version_id; returns versionId
- lockDraft(scriptId, sagaRunId) sets status=locked atomically via SELECT FOR UPDATE; returns ALREADY_EXISTS if locked by a different sagaRunId
- unlockDraft(scriptId, sagaRunId) verifies sagaRunId match; sets status=draft; clears saga_run_id
- All gRPC methods return standard gRPC status codes: NOT_FOUND, PERMISSION_DENIED, ALREADY_EXISTS, INVALID_ARGUMENT as appropriate
- ScriptService proto in packages/proto/script.proto; buf generate produces TypeScript stubs with zero compile errors
Parent: Script Service Core

Title: Implement upsertASTNodes gRPC method with batch INSERT ON CONFLICT for up to 500 nodes
Description: Implement ScriptService.UpsertASTNodes in services/script/src/handlers/ast.handler.ts. SQL template using pg-format for batch: INSERT INTO script.ast_nodes (id, script_id, parent_id, node_type, position, metadata, revision_color, updated_at) VALUES %L ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE SET parent_id = EXCLUDED.parent_id, node_type = EXCLUDED.node_type, position = EXCLUDED.position, metadata = EXCLUDED.metadata, revision_color = EXCLUDED.revision_color, updated_at = now(). Validate node_type against the 19 valid types in packages/ast NodeType enum before insert. Validate script_id belongs to the caller's org via a single SELECT join. Chunk requests larger than 500 nodes into sequential batches of 500. Performance test in CI: insert 100 nodes → measure p99 latency → assert under 500ms. Integration test: insert 100 nodes, call again updating 50 of them, verify both operations produce correct final state in DB.
Parent: As a service consumer the Script Service provides gRPC APIs for AST node management version snapshots and draft lifecycle control

Title: Implement getSceneSnapshot gRPC method returning computed scene metadata from ast_nodes
Description: Implement ScriptService.GetSceneSnapshot in services/script/src/handlers/snapshot.handler.ts. Logic: (1) Query ast_nodes WHERE id = sceneId AND node_type = 'scene' — return NOT_FOUND if missing. (2) Query ast_nodes WHERE parent_id = sceneId ORDER BY position to get child elements. (3) Extract headingText from child where node_type = 'scene_heading'; read from metadata.textContent. (4) Build elementCounts: count children by node_type grouping: { action, dialogue, character, parenthetical, transition, general }. (5) Estimate page count: SUM of element line heights where action/general = 1 line per 70 chars, dialogue = 1 line per 35 chars, character/parenthetical/transition = 1 line; divide total by 55 (lines per page); round to nearest 0.125. (6) Return sceneNumber from metadata.sceneNumber. Unit test with mock DB rows: verify elementCounts and estimatedPageCount calculated correctly for known inputs.
Parent: As a service consumer the Script Service provides gRPC APIs for AST node management version snapshots and draft lifecycle control

Title: Implement createVersion gRPC method storing Loro CRDT binary snapshot in script_versions
Description: Implement ScriptService.CreateVersion in services/script/src/handlers/version.handler.ts. Logic: (1) Validate snapshotBytes is not null and size does not exceed 50MB (return INVALID_ARGUMENT if exceeded). (2) Generate id as UUIDv7 (time-sortable — use uuid package v9 uuid() with timestamp prefix). (3) INSERT INTO script.script_versions (id, script_id, version_label, crdt_snapshot, created_by, created_at) VALUES (…). (4) UPDATE script.scripts SET current_version_id = newVersionId, updated_at = now() WHERE id = scriptId. (5) Return { versionId, scriptId, createdAt }. Both DB operations in a single transaction. Integration test: create script → call createVersion with 1KB fake snapshot → verify script.current_version_id updated → call createVersion again → verify second versionId created and current_version_id updated to second version → verify first version still exists in script_versions.
Parent: As a service consumer the Script Service provides gRPC APIs for AST node management version snapshots and draft lifecycle control

Title: Implement lockDraft and unlockDraft gRPC methods with pessimistic row-level locking
Description: Implement ScriptService.LockDraft and ScriptService.UnlockDraft in services/script/src/handlers/lifecycle.handler.ts. LockDraft: (1) BEGIN TRANSACTION; (2) SELECT id, status, saga_run_id FROM script.scripts WHERE id = scriptId FOR UPDATE NOWAIT — if another transaction holds the lock return UNAVAILABLE gRPC status; (3) If status != 'draft': return ALREADY_EXISTS with message 'Script already locked by saga {existing_saga_run_id}'; (4) UPDATE scripts SET status = 'locked', saga_run_id = request.sagaRunId, updated_at = now() WHERE id = scriptId; (5) COMMIT. UnlockDraft: (1) BEGIN; (2) SELECT status, saga_run_id WHERE id = scriptId FOR UPDATE NOWAIT; (3) If saga_run_id != request.sagaRunId: return PERMISSION_DENIED; (4) UPDATE scripts SET status = 'draft', saga_run_id = NULL, updated_at = now(); (5) COMMIT. Integration test: lockDraft → attempt second lockDraft → verify ALREADY_EXISTS error → unlockDraft → verify status='draft'.
Parent: As a service consumer the Script Service provides gRPC APIs for AST node management version snapshots and draft lifecycle control

Title: Define complete ScriptService proto definitions in packages/proto/script.proto with all RPC methods and message types
Description: Write packages/proto/script.proto defining all 11 ScriptService RPC methods. Service: service ScriptService { rpc CreateProject returns CreateProjectResponse; rpc ListProjects returns ListProjectsResponse; rpc ArchiveProject returns google.protobuf.Empty; rpc CreateScript returns CreateScriptResponse; rpc GetScript returns ScriptResponse; rpc ListScripts returns ListScriptsResponse; rpc UpsertASTNodes returns UpsertASTNodesResponse; rpc GetSceneSnapshot returns SceneSnapshotResponse; rpc CreateVersion returns CreateVersionResponse; rpc LockDraft returns LockDraftResponse; rpc UnlockDraft returns google.protobuf.Empty; } Include ASTNodeProto message mirroring all 19 node types from packages/ast NodeType enum. Include SceneSnapshotResponse with ElementCounts sub-message. Add all messages for requests and responses. Update buf.gen.yaml to include script.proto. Run buf generate; verify generated TypeScript stubs compile with zero errors in packages/proto. Check proto file into version control.
Parent: As a service consumer the Script Service provides gRPC APIs for AST node management version snapshots and draft lifecycle control

Title: As a developer the Script Service is deployed to staging integrated with NATS and the Apollo Router supergraph
Description: Once the Script Service gRPC and GraphQL are implemented it must be containerised, deployed to the GKE staging cluster, and wired into the event bus and API gateway. The NATS script.checkpointed event is the primary integration point consumed by Breakdown and Search services. This story covers operational readiness: Docker image, Kubernetes manifests, Atlas migration job, and NATS publisher verification. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 2 Definition of Done.
Acceptance Criteria:
- script.checkpointed NATS JetStream event published with payload { scriptId, orgId, changedSceneIds, versionId, timestamp } within 1s of each createVersion call
- NATS subject: scriptos.script.checkpointed; stream: SCRIPT_EVENTS; retention: WorkQueuePolicy; durable consumers: breakdown, search pre-created
- Script Service Docker image built in CI; multi-stage node:20-alpine runtime; compressed image size under 100MB
- Kubernetes Deployment: 2 replicas, readinessProbe on gRPC health check port 4001, resource limits 500m CPU / 512Mi RAM
- Kubernetes Service: ClusterIP exposing port 4001 (gRPC) and port 4002 (GraphQL HTTP)
- /health HTTP endpoint at port 4002 returns { status: 'ok', version: '<git-sha>' }
- Istio sidecar injected; mTLS active on all inbound gRPC traffic from within the mesh
- Atlas migration Kubernetes Job runs before deployment; apply is idempotent and does not fail on re-deploy
Parent: Script Service Core

Title: Implement script.checkpointed NATS JetStream event publisher in the Script Service
Description: Implement NATS event publishing in services/script/src/events/checkpoint.publisher.ts. Import DomainEvent<T> envelope from packages/events. Define ScriptCheckpointedPayload: { scriptId: string; orgId: string; changedSceneIds: string[]; versionId: string; checkpointedAt: string }. Create CheckpointPublisher class with publish(payload: ScriptCheckpointedPayload): Promise<void> method. Use NATS JetStream js.publish() — not core NATS publish — so the message is persisted in the SCRIPT_EVENTS stream. Await the PubAck confirmation before returning (do not fire-and-forget). Retry policy: on PubAck timeout retry up to 3 times with 200ms/500ms/1000ms backoff. If all retries fail: INSERT into script.failed_events(subject, payload, error, created_at) for dead-letter replay. Call publish() from the createVersion handler after successful DB commit. Integration test: call createVersion via gRPC → subscribe to scriptos.script.checkpointed → assert message received with correct scriptId within 2s.
Parent: As a developer the Script Service is deployed to staging integrated with NATS and the Apollo Router supergraph

Title: Containerise Script Service and deploy to GKE staging with Kubernetes Deployment Service and Atlas migration Job
Description: (1) Create services/script/Dockerfile extending infra/docker/Dockerfile.service with ARG SERVICE=script. Verify multi-stage build produces node:20-alpine runtime image under 100MB. (2) Create infra/k8s/services/script/deployment.yaml: 2 replicas; image tag from CI (GIT_SHA arg); env from ExternalSecret (DATABASE_URL, NATS_URL, REDIS_URL, GRPC_PORT=4001, HTTP_PORT=4002); resource limits 500m CPU / 512Mi RAM; readinessProbe grpc on port 4001; livenessProbe httpGet /health on port 4002; terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30. (3) Create infra/k8s/services/script/service.yaml: ClusterIP; port 4001 name grpc, port 4002 name http. (4) Create infra/k8s/services/script/migration-job.yaml: Kubernetes Job image=atlassian/atlas:latest running atlas schema apply --url $DATABASE_URL --to file://schema.hcl; completions: 1; backoffLimit: 3; add as an Argo pre-sync hook or run via CI before image deploy. (5) Add script-service to .github/workflows/release.yml Docker push step. Deploy and verify kubectl get pods -l app=script-service returns 2/2 Running.
Parent: As a developer the Script Service is deployed to staging integrated with NATS and the Apollo Router supergraph

Title: Script Editor Web UI Sprint 2
Description: The Sprint 2 frontend delivers the project and script navigation layer: the pages writers use before entering the editor. Writers need a clear starting point — view their projects, create a new one, navigate into scripts, and open the editor placeholder. This UI layer establishes the React page structure, routing, and Apollo Client codegen hooks that the Sprint 3 real editor will build directly on top of. All components must use the graphql-codegen generated types so the full type chain (GraphQL schema → TypeScript) is validated from day one. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 2 frontend tasks.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Project list page renders all org projects with title, type badge, script count, and relative updated time
- Empty state (no projects) shows an illustration with a Create your first project CTA
- Create project modal allows name + type selection and submits via GraphQL mutation; new project appears immediately in list
- Script list page within a project shows all scripts with title and status badge
- Create script modal creates a new script and navigates to /scripts/{id}/editor which renders a placeholder for Sprint 2
- All routes are protected; unauthenticated users redirected to /login
- graphql-codegen generates TypeScript types from all queries/mutations; zero type errors
Parent: Script Service & Collaborative Editor

Title: As a writer I can browse my projects and scripts and navigate to the screenplay editor from the web app
Description: The project and script browsing UI is the writer's home base. It must be fast and intuitive with clear CTAs for creating a first project. The Apollo Client hooks established here are reused throughout the app. Empty states must guide users rather than showing blank screens. The route structure established in this sprint — /projects, /projects/:id/scripts, /scripts/:id/editor — must be correct from the start to avoid routing refactors in Sprint 3. Reference: Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 2 frontend tasks.
Acceptance Criteria:
- /projects page loads project list within 2s; shows project title, type badge (colour-coded), script count, and last updated date
- Empty state (no projects): illustration + 'Create your first project' button
- Create project modal: title input (required, max 100 chars), scriptType select with all ProjectType options; Submit calls createProject mutation; new project appears in list via Apollo cache update (optimistic UI)
- Clicking a project navigates to /projects/{id}/scripts showing script list
- Script list: ScriptCard with title, status badge (Draft=blue, Locked=orange, Archived=grey), updatedAt relative time
- Create script modal: title input + scriptType select; Submit calls createScript mutation; navigates to /scripts/{id}/editor
- /scripts/:id/editor renders Sprint 2 placeholder: shows script title loaded via GraphQL query + 'Editor coming in Sprint 3' message
- All pages wrapped in ProtectedRoute; unauthenticated access redirects to /login with return URL in state
- Apollo InMemoryCache updated on mutations; no full page reload needed
Parent: Script Editor Web UI Sprint 2

Title: Build create script modal with GraphQL mutation and editor route navigation placeholder
Description: Create src/components/CreateScriptModal.tsx using React Hook Form + Zod. Fields: title (required, max 100 chars), scriptType Select. Submit calls useMutation(CREATE_SCRIPT) with projectId from route params. On success: navigate to /scripts/{newId}/editor using React Router useNavigate. Create src/pages/EditorPage.tsx as a Sprint 2 placeholder: load script(id) query to get title; render a centred page with the script title as heading, a film slate icon, and 'Full collaborative editor arriving in Sprint 3' subtext. Route /scripts/:scriptId/editor → EditorPage in React Router. This establishes the correct route now so Sprint 3 only replaces the EditorPage body — no routing changes needed. Vitest test: render EditorPage with mocked script query returning { id, title: 'My Script' }; assert title appears in the DOM.
Parent: As a writer I can browse my projects and scripts and navigate to the screenplay editor from the web app

Title: CRDT Collaboration Service
Description: The Collaboration Service powers real-time multi-user screenplay editing. It runs a Hocuspocus WebSocket server (the startup-phase CRDT sync server, upgraded to y-redis in Sprint 18) that manages Loro CRDT documents in memory with Redis persistence. Each scriptId maps to a LoroDoc instance. Client changes are applied via CRDT ops, synced to all connected clients, and periodically checkpointed to the Script Service (ast_nodes + script_versions). Structural operations (scene move, split, merge) are handled as atomic Loro MovableTree operations with a Redis coordination lock to prevent concurrent structural conflicts. This is the highest-risk sprint — the Loro POC gate at Sprint 3 Week 1 must pass before implementation proceeds. Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 3, ADR-001, ADR-018, ADR-019.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Loro POC gate passed: all 5 criteria from Wiki/04 POC Gate passed and decision recorded before implementation begins
- Two clients editing the same script converge to identical content within 200ms under normal network conditions
- Network partition test: client edits offline for 5 minutes; on reconnect edits merge correctly with zero data loss
- Checkpoint: after 30s idle, ast_nodes updated via Script Service gRPC; script.checkpointed NATS event published
- Doc load cold path (Redis miss): loads snapshot from Postgres via Script Service gRPC; populates Redis; ready in under 2s
- Doc load warm path (Redis hit): loads Loro binary from Redis; ready in under 100ms
- Scene move: concurrent same-scene moves produce deterministic result; no scenes lost
- Scene split and merge: atomic; no intermediate states visible to other clients
- Awareness: cursor positions and collaborator presence visible within 100ms of change
- Collaboration Service deployed to staging; 10 concurrent WS connections stable for 30 minutes
Parent: Script Service & Collaborative Editor

Title: The Loro CRDT POC validates the library choice against 5 gate criteria before full implementation begins
Description: The choice of Loro as the primary CRDT library (ADR-001, ADR-018) is the highest-risk architectural decision in the platform. Before committing to the full Collaboration Service implementation the team must run a focused proof-of-concept validating Loro against 5 specific criteria from Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md. The POC must be completed by Sprint 3 Day 3 so there is time to activate the Yjs fallback within the sprint if needed. Results must be documented in the wiki before any implementation work begins. Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md POC Gate, ADR-001, ADR-018.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Criterion 1 (Text Convergence): 3 Loro Doc instances apply concurrent insertions and deletions to the same text range; after bidirectional merge all 3 instances have identical text with no characters lost or duplicated
- Criterion 2 (MovableTree Convergence): 2 instances concurrently move the same tree node to different parent positions; after merge both instances have identical deterministic tree structure with no node duplication or loss
- Criterion 3 (Network Partition): 1 client applies 1000 offline ops; reconnects; all ops merge correctly with a second online client; final state identical on both sides; merge completes in under 1s
- Criterion 4 (Performance): 100K sequential text insertions applied to a single LoroDoc in under 2s; memory increase under 200MB; snapshot export and import both under 500ms
- Criterion 5 (ProseMirror Binding): prototype LoroPMBinding converts Loro text delta to PM transaction and back in a 10-step round-trip without infinite loops
- POC source code in packages/ast/loro-poc/; results documented in Wiki/04 POC Gate Results with explicit pass/fail per criterion
- Go/no-go decision recorded: Loro passes all 5 → proceed; 2+ failures → Yjs fallback activated per Wiki/04 Yjs Fallback Plan
Parent: CRDT Collaboration Service

Title: Run Loro concurrent text edit convergence test for POC Criterion 1 (text convergence)
Description: Write packages/ast/loro-poc/text-convergence.test.ts using Vitest. Setup: create 3 LoroDoc instances (A, B, C) from a common snapshot containing a single LoroText node with text 'The quick brown fox'. Simulate concurrent edits without seeing each other: A inserts ' lazy' before 'fox' at position 16; B deletes 'quick ' (positions 4-10) and inserts 'slow '; C appends ' jumped over' at end. Exchange updates: A imports B's and C's updates; B imports A's and C's; C imports A's and B's (use doc.exportFrom(commonVersionVector) for minimal update payloads). Assert: A.getText() === B.getText() === C.getText(). Print the final merged text. Run via pnpm test --filter=loro-poc. Record exact merged text and pass/fail in Wiki/04 POC Gate Results. POC must run in CI (no network calls).
Parent: The Loro CRDT POC validates the library choice against 5 gate criteria before full implementation begins

Title: Run Loro MovableTree concurrent scene move test for POC Criterion 2 (structural convergence)
Description: Write packages/ast/loro-poc/movable-tree.test.ts. Setup: create a LoroMovableList (or LoroTree — identify the correct Loro API for an ordered movable tree) on 2 LoroDoc instances (A, B) representing 5 scene nodes s1..s5 as children of a root. Concurrent ops without seeing each other: A moves s3 to be a child of s1; B moves s3 to be a child of s4 (same node, different target). Exchange updates bidirectionally. Assert: (1) A and B have identical final tree structure; (2) s3 appears at exactly one position (no duplication); (3) all other nodes s1, s2, s4, s5 are still present and unaffected. Also test a second scenario: A and B both move different nodes (A moves s2, B moves s4) — assert both moves are applied. Document which Loro API was used (LoroMovableList vs LoroTree vs LoroMap tree encoding) and the exact determinism semantics observed. Record pass/fail in Wiki/04.
Parent: The Loro CRDT POC validates the library choice against 5 gate criteria before full implementation begins

Title: Run Loro network partition reconnect test for POC Criterion 3 (offline resilience)
Description: Write packages/ast/loro-poc/partition.test.ts. Setup: Doc A and Doc B start from a common snapshot. Partition: Doc A applies 1000 sequential single-character insertions (simulate 1000 keystrokes offline). Doc B applies 20 word insertions in 20 separate operations (simulate another writer working online). Reconnect: exchange all updates bidirectionally (A.import(B.exportFrom(commonVector)); B.import(A.exportFrom(commonVector))). Assert: (1) A.getText() === B.getText() — content identical. (2) All 1000 of A's inserted characters are present in merged text (no data loss). (3) Merge operation completes in under 1000ms — measure with Date.now(). Record exact merge time and pass/fail in Wiki/04. Also record total byte size of A's 1000-op update payload as a proxy for sync bandwidth cost.
Parent: The Loro CRDT POC validates the library choice against 5 gate criteria before full implementation begins

Title: Run Loro performance benchmark for POC Criterion 4 (100K ops under 2s and memory under 200MB)
Description: Write packages/ast/loro-poc/performance.bench.ts using Vitest bench API (bench() function). Benchmark 1 — sequential inserts: create LoroDoc with a 1000-char text node; apply 100K single-character insertions at position end. Measure total time via performance.now(); assert under 2000ms. Benchmark 2 — memory: record process.memoryUsage().heapUsed before and after; assert increase under 200MB. Benchmark 3 — snapshot export: after 100K ops call doc.export({ mode: 'snapshot' }); measure time; record snapshot byte size. Benchmark 4 — snapshot import: create fresh LoroDoc; call doc.import(snapshot); measure time; assert import under 500ms. Benchmark 5 — update export: call doc.exportFrom(emptyVector) for incremental update; measure size vs full snapshot. Run benchmarks on CI runner spec (4 CPU, 8GB RAM). Record all numbers in Wiki/04 POC Gate Results. Fail CI step if 100K-insert benchmark exceeds 2000ms.
Parent: The Loro CRDT POC validates the library choice against 5 gate criteria before full implementation begins

Title: Prototype LoroPMBinding class and run ProseMirror round-trip test for POC Criterion 5
Description: Write packages/ast/loro-poc/pm-binding.test.ts. Prototype a minimal LoroPMBinding class with two methods: (1) applyLoroOpToPM(delta: LoroDelta, pmState: EditorState): Transaction — maps Loro text delta (array of {retain?, insert?, delete?} ops) to a ProseMirror Transaction using tr.insertText and tr.delete at the resolved positions. (2) applyPMTransactionToLoro(tr: Transaction, loroText: LoroText): void — maps ProseMirror ReplaceStep to Loro loroText.insert/delete. Loop guard: use a boolean flag applyingRemote to prevent the subscription from re-applying the reflected op. Test (10-step round-trip): (1) PM editor state with paragraph 'Hello'. (2) PM insert ' World' → applyPMTransactionToLoro → assert loroText = 'Hello World'. (3) Loro insert ' Foo' → applyLoroOpToPM → apply to PM → assert PM content = 'Hello World Foo'. (4) Repeat 4 more cycles. (5) Assert no infinite loop (max 10 PM transactions fired). Record binding approach and pass/fail in Wiki/04. This code becomes the foundation of the production LoroPMBinding in Sprint 3.
Tags: crdt; loro; prosemirror; tiptap; poc; sprint-3
Parent: The Loro CRDT POC validates the library choice against 5 gate criteria before full implementation begins

Title: As writers we can co-edit a screenplay in real-time with changes syncing reliably between all connected clients
Description: Real-time collaborative editing is the core differentiator of ScriptOS over standalone tools. The Collaboration Service must accept WebSocket connections, maintain a Loro CRDT document per script, sync ops between clients in real time, and periodically checkpoint the CRDT state to the Script Service. The service uses Hocuspocus as the WebSocket CRDT server (startup phase; upgraded to y-redis for horizontal scaling in Sprint 18). The Redis-backed document store ensures fast cold starts and survives pod restarts. Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md Startup Architecture, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 3.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Two browser tabs open the same script; typing in tab A appears in tab B within 200ms; convergence verified with automated Playwright test
- Offline simulation: disconnect tab A, type 10 lines, reconnect; all 10 lines appear in tab B within 3s of reconnect
- Checkpoint triggers after 30 seconds of inactivity; ast_nodes in Script Service updated; script.checkpointed NATS event published within 5s of checkpoint
- Cold start (Redis miss): loads Loro snapshot from Postgres via Script Service gRPC; populates Redis; document ready in under 2s
- Warm start (Redis hit): loads Loro binary from Redis; document ready in under 100ms
- WebSocket gateway correctly routes /collaboration/{scriptId} upgrades to the Collaboration Service pod with sticky sessions
- CheckpointCRDT gRPC method callable externally; triggers immediate checkpoint cycle
Parent: CRDT Collaboration Service

Title: Set up Hocuspocus server in the Collaboration Service with JWT authentication and Loro document management
Description: Create services/collaboration/src/server.ts. Install: @hocuspocus/server, loro-crdt. Configure HocuspocusServer: (1) onAuthenticate({ token }): parse JWT from token field; verify RS256 signature using JWKS endpoint from Auth Service (cache JWKS with 5-minute TTL); on valid JWT return { user: { id, orgId, roles } }; on invalid JWT throw Forbidden. (2) onLoadDocument({ documentName, context }): documentName = scriptId; call loadDocState(scriptId, context.user.orgId) — see task #100; if snapshot exists call doc.import(snapshot); return doc. (3) onStoreDocument({ documentName, document }): debounced 30s using lodash debounce; call checkpoint(document, documentName, orgId) — see task #101. (4) onChange: Hocuspocus broadcasts updates to all connected clients automatically. (5) onConnect/onDisconnect: update in-memory presence map { scriptId → Set<userId> }. Server listens port 4003. Add HTTP health endpoint Express server on port 4004 returning { status: ok }. Create services/collaboration/src/index.ts bootstrapping both servers.
Parent: As writers we can co-edit a screenplay in real-time with changes syncing reliably between all connected clients

Title: Implement loadDocState() with Redis hot path and Postgres cold start fallback in the Collaboration Service
Description: Implement services/collaboration/src/doc-store.ts. Export loadDocState(scriptId: string, orgId: string): Promise<Uint8Array | null>. Logic: (1) const redisKey = 'collab:doc:' + scriptId; (2) Try redis.getBuffer(redisKey) — if hit: return Buffer as Uint8Array (log cache hit + latency). (3) On Redis miss: call Script Service gRPC GetLatestVersion(scriptId) via the ScriptService gRPC client. If version found: set Redis with 24h TTL: await redis.set(redisKey, snapshotBytes, 'EX', 86400); return snapshotBytes. (4) If no version (new script): return null — Hocuspocus initialises a fresh LoroDoc. Export saveDocState(scriptId: string, snapshot: Uint8Array): Promise<void> — SET with 24h TTL. Called after each checkpoint to keep Redis warm. Unit tests: (a) Redis hit → assert gRPC not called; (b) Redis miss → assert gRPC called → assert Redis populated with correct TTL; (c) gRPC NOT_FOUND → assert returns null.
Parent: As writers we can co-edit a screenplay in real-time with changes syncing reliably between all connected clients

Title: Implement checkpoint() extracting AST from Loro doc writing to Script Service and publishing NATS event
Description: Implement services/collaboration/src/checkpoint.ts. Export checkpoint(doc: LoroDoc, scriptId: string, orgId: string): Promise<void>. Logic: (1) const snapshot = doc.export({ mode: 'snapshot' }) as Uint8Array. (2) Extract AST nodes: traverse the Loro MovableTree (the root tree in the doc representing the screenplay structure); for each node collect { id, parentId, nodeType, position, metadata }. For leaf text nodes read content via doc.getText(nodeId).toString() and store in metadata.textContent. Build ASTNodeProto array. (3) Call ScriptService.UpsertASTNodes with the full node array (chunk at 500). Collect changedSceneIds (scene nodes where content changed since last checkpoint — compare against Redis-cached previousSnapshot if available). (4) Call ScriptService.CreateVersion with snapshot bytes; receive versionId. (5) Call saveDocState(scriptId, snapshot). (6) Publish script.checkpointed NATS event. Error handling: if UpsertASTNodes fails retry 3 times with backoff; on persistent failure log to dead-letter table; do not block CRDT sync (checkpoint is async). Integration test: create LoroDoc with 3 scene nodes → call checkpoint() → verify 3+ ast_nodes rows in DB → verify NATS event received.
Parent: As writers we can co-edit a screenplay in real-time with changes syncing reliably between all connected clients

Title: Update WebSocket gateway to route collaboration connections by scriptId with org validation and sticky sessions
Description: Update services/collaboration/src/ws-gateway.ts. On WS upgrade request: (1) Parse URL path: expect /collaboration/{scriptId}; validate scriptId is valid UUID v7 format — close 4003 if not. (2) After JWT validation (already implemented in Sprint 1 stub) verify script ownership: call ScriptService.GetScript(scriptId) gRPC; confirm script.orgId === jwtClaims.orgId — close 4001 if mismatch. (3) Proxy the WebSocket connection to the Collaboration Service on port 4003 using the http-proxy-middleware wsProxy utility — forward all frames bidirectionally. The gateway is stateless: it proxies; it does not hold any CRDT state. Update infra/k8s/services/collaboration/service.yaml to add sessionAffinity: ClientIP with timeoutSeconds: 10800 (3h) — this ensures a user's WS connection always reaches the same Hocuspocus pod where their doc is loaded. Update Istio VirtualService to use consistent hash load balancing on the x-user-id header for additional routing stability. Document sticky session approach in Wiki/04.
Parent: As writers we can co-edit a screenplay in real-time with changes syncing reliably between all connected clients

Title: Define CollaborationService proto with CheckpointCRDT and presence methods in packages/proto
Description: Write packages/proto/collaboration.proto. Service: service CollaborationService { rpc CheckpointCRDT(CheckpointCRDTRequest) returns (CheckpointCRDTResponse); rpc GetDocumentState(GetDocumentStateRequest) returns (GetDocumentStateResponse); rpc GetPresence(GetPresenceRequest) returns (GetPresenceResponse); rpc HandleStructuralOp(HandleStructuralOpRequest) returns (HandleStructuralOpResponse); } Messages: CheckpointCRDTRequest { string script_id = 1; string org_id = 2; }; CheckpointCRDTResponse { string version_id = 1; int64 nodes_written = 2; }; GetDocumentStateResponse { bytes snapshot = 1; string version_id = 2; int64 snapshot_size_bytes = 3; }; PresenceEntry { string user_id = 1; string user_name = 2; string revision_color = 3; CursorPosition cursor = 4; bool is_active = 5; }; HandleStructuralOpRequest { string script_id = 1; oneof op { SceneMoveOp move = 2; SceneSplitOp split = 3; SceneMergeOp merge = 4; } }. Run buf generate; verify TypeScript stubs compile with zero errors. Add collaboration.proto to buf.gen.yaml.
Parent: As writers we can co-edit a screenplay in real-time with changes syncing reliably between all connected clients

Title: As writers structural screenplay operations (move split and merge scenes) remain consistent across all connected clients
Description: Beyond text editing writers frequently reorganise scripts by moving scenes, splitting a scene at a natural break, or merging two short scenes. These structural operations must be safe under concurrent access. They are handled as atomic Loro MovableTree operations with a Redis coordination lock to prevent concurrent structural conflicts that CRDT alone cannot resolve deterministically (two writers moving the same scene to different positions). Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md Structural Operations, ADR-019.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Scene move: moving a scene from position 3 to position 7 while another writer types in scene 5; after sync all clients see scene at position 7 with the typing intact
- Concurrent different-scene moves: A moves scene 2, B moves scene 4 simultaneously; both moves applied; no scenes lost
- Concurrent same-scene moves (conflict): A and B both try to move scene 3; Redis lock ensures one wins; losing client receives CONCURRENT_STRUCTURAL_OP error and sees the winner's move applied within 500ms
- Scene split at a specific child node: scene 1 (5 child nodes) split after node 3 creates scene 1 (nodes 1-3) and scene 2 (nodes 4-5); new scene has empty heading; all clients see both scenes within 500ms
- Scene merge: scenes 3 and 4 merged; result has concatenated content in correct order; scene 4 node removed; all clients converge within 500ms
- All structural ops are represented as discrete AST node mutations in the next checkpoint cycle
Parent: CRDT Collaboration Service

Title: Implement handleSceneMoveOp with Redis distributed lock acquisition and release for concurrent safety
Description: Implement services/collaboration/src/structural/scene-move.ts. Export handleSceneMoveOp(doc: LoroDoc, op: SceneMoveOp, scriptId: string, redis: Redis, hocuspocus: HocuspocusServer): Promise<SceneMoveResult>. SceneMoveOp = { sceneId: string; newParentId: string; newPosition: number; requestedByUserId: string }. Logic: (1) Acquire Redis lock: SET lock:structural:{scriptId} {requestedByUserId} NX EX 5 (5s TTL, NX=only if absent). Poll up to 2s with 100ms interval — if not acquired return { success: false, error: 'CONCURRENT_STRUCTURAL_OP' }. (2) Inside lock: use Loro MovableTree API to move sceneId to newParentId at newPosition within a Loro transaction (batch). (3) Broadcast the Loro update binary to all connected clients via hocuspocus.broadcastStateless (or equivalent). (4) Release lock: DEL lock:structural:{scriptId}. (5) Return { success: true, finalPosition }. Use try/finally to ensure lock release even on error. Integration test: two concurrent move ops on same scriptId; verify only one acquires lock; other returns CONCURRENT_STRUCTURAL_OP; final Loro doc state is consistent.
Parent: As writers structural screenplay operations (move split and merge scenes) remain consistent across all connected clients

Title: Implement scene split as decomposed atomic Loro MovableTree operations with Redis structural lock
Description: Implement services/collaboration/src/structural/scene-split.ts. Export handleSceneSplitOp(doc: LoroDoc, op: SceneSplitOp, scriptId: string, redis: Redis, hocuspocus: HocuspocusServer): Promise<StructuralOpResult>. SceneSplitOp = { sceneId: string; splitAfterChildId: string; newSceneId: string }. Logic (inside Redis structural lock — acquire same lock:structural:{scriptId} pattern as scene-move): (1) Within a single Loro transaction batch: (a) Collect ordered child nodes of sceneId from MovableTree. (b) Partition into beforeNodes (up to and including splitAfterChildId) and afterNodes (remainder). (c) Insert newSceneId as a new sibling immediately after sceneId in the parent tree. (d) Move each afterNode from sceneId to newSceneId using MovableTree move ops (position = index). (e) Create a new scene_heading child node under newSceneId with empty headingText. (2) Broadcast update. (3) Release lock. Atomic Loro batch ensures no intermediate states are observable. Integration test: scene with 5 children; split after child 3; verify sceneId has children 1-3; newSceneId has children 4-5 plus new heading.
Parent: As writers structural screenplay operations (move split and merge scenes) remain consistent across all connected clients

Title: Implement scene merge as atomic MovableTree content concatenation and source node removal
Description: Implement services/collaboration/src/structural/scene-merge.ts. Export handleSceneMergeOp(doc: LoroDoc, op: SceneMergeOp, scriptId: string, redis: Redis, hocuspocus: HocuspocusServer): Promise<StructuralOpResult>. SceneMergeOp = { sourceSceneId: string; targetSceneId: string }. Validation (before acquiring lock): verify sourceSceneId and targetSceneId share the same parent node in the Loro tree — return INVALID_ARGUMENT if not siblings. Logic (inside Redis structural lock, single Loro transaction): (1) Get ordered children of sourceScene. (2) Get current children count of targetScene (to compute append positions). (3) Move each sourceScene child to targetScene at position (targetScene.childCount + index) using MovableTree move ops — preserving content order. (4) Delete the sourceScene node from the MovableTree (and its scene_heading child which was not moved — scene_heading is implicitly removed with the scene). (5) Broadcast update. (6) Release lock. Integration test: 2 scenes with 3 content nodes each; merge; verify 1 scene with 6 content nodes in correct order; source scene no longer exists in tree.
Parent: As writers structural screenplay operations (move split and merge scenes) remain consistent across all connected clients

Title: As a writer I can see other collaborators cursor positions and presence in real time using the awareness protocol
Description: Seeing where other writers are working reduces merge anxiety and enables real-time coordination. The awareness protocol broadcasts cursor positions and user metadata (name, revision color) to all connected clients using Hocuspocus awareness state — which is ephemeral (not stored in Postgres or Redis). Disconnected users must be removed promptly so stale cursors do not confuse collaborators. Revision colors from the 13-color WGA palette are assigned on connect. Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md Awareness Protocol, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 3.
Acceptance Criteria:
- User A types in scene 3; User B (different browser tab) sees User A's cursor at the correct position within 100ms
- User A's cursor label shows User A's name and revision color from the WGA 13-color palette
- When User A closes their tab, User A's cursor disappears from User B's view within 5 seconds
- Presence avatars in the editor toolbar show all currently connected collaborators
- Awareness state is ephemeral — not persisted in Postgres or Redis; lost on pod restart (acceptable)
- Revision colors are assigned round-robin from RevisionColor enum in packages/ast on connect; stable for the session duration
- A single user connected from two tabs gets two separate awareness entries (different socket IDs)
Parent: CRDT Collaboration Service

Title: Implement awareness protocol for cursor position and user presence state in the Hocuspocus server
Description: Implement services/collaboration/src/awareness.ts. On connect (inside onAuthenticate callback): (1) Assign revision color: maintain in-memory map colorAssignments: Map<scriptId, Map<userId, RevisionColor>>. On first connect for a scriptId assign RevisionColor values round-robin using the 13 values from packages/ast RevisionColor enum (skip WHITE which is reserved for unassigned). (2) Set initial awareness: hocuspocus will expose an awareness API; set { userId, userName: context.user.name, revisionColor, cursor: null } on connection. On awareness update from client (cursor movement): Hocuspocus broadcasts to all other connected clients automatically — verify this works natively with Loro's awareness module. If Loro requires a custom awareness implementation (not Y.js awareness): implement a simple broadcast: on message from client matching { type: 'awareness', cursor: {...} }, broadcast to all other sockets on same scriptId. On disconnect (onDisconnect): clear colorAssignment for this socket. Integration test: 2 WS connections same scriptId; connection 1 sends awareness update; assert connection 2 receives awareness within 100ms. Disconnect connection 1; assert connection 2 receives updated presence within 5s.
Parent: As a writer I can see other collaborators cursor positions and presence in real time using the awareness protocol

Title: Deploy Collaboration Service to GKE staging with sticky session Kubernetes Service and load test
Description: (1) Create services/collaboration/Dockerfile — multi-stage node:20-alpine; expose ports 4003 (Hocuspocus WS) and 4004 (health HTTP). (2) Create infra/k8s/services/collaboration/deployment.yaml: 2 replicas; image from GCR; env from ExternalSecret (REDIS_URL, SCRIPT_SERVICE_GRPC_URL, NATS_URL); resource limits: 1 CPU / 1Gi RAM (higher than script-service due to in-memory Loro docs per connected script); readinessProbe httpGet /health port 4004; livenessProbe httpGet /health; terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60 (allow checkpoints to flush on shutdown — implement SIGTERM handler that triggers immediate checkpoint on all open docs). (3) infra/k8s/services/collaboration/service.yaml: ClusterIP; sessionAffinity: ClientIP; sessionAffinityConfig.clientIP.timeoutSeconds: 10800. (4) Update Istio DestinationRule for collaboration-service: add trafficPolicy.loadBalancer.consistentHash on httpHeaderName: x-user-id for additional routing stability. (5) Load test in CI: 10 concurrent WS connections for 30 minutes; assert no pod restarts; assert memory under 800Mi per pod.
Parent: As a writer I can see other collaborators cursor positions and presence in real time using the awareness protocol

Title: Screenplay Editor UI
Description: The Screenplay Editor UI is the primary product surface that writers interact with. Built on TipTap (ProseMirror) with a custom screenplay schema defining 7 element types (Scene Heading, Action, Character, Dialogue, Parenthetical, Transition, General), it connects to the Collaboration Service via WebSocket and binds Loro CRDT ops to ProseMirror transactions via the LoroPMBinding class. Visual output must match Final Draft-quality formatting: Courier New 12pt, industry-standard margins, uppercase scene headings and character names, and page break indicators every 55 lines. The dogfood milestone (Week 8) is gated on this feature: the engineering team must be able to write real scripts in ScriptOS. Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md ProseMirror Binding, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 3 frontend tasks.
Acceptance Criteria:
- TipTap editor renders all 7 screenplay element types with correct visual formatting
- LoroPMBinding established: typing in the editor creates Loro ops; remote Loro ops render as ProseMirror transactions; no infinite loops
- Tab key cycles element type: scene_heading → action → character → dialogue → parenthetical; Shift+Tab cycles in reverse
- Screenplay formatting: Courier New 12pt, 1.5in left / 1in right margins, page break indicators at every 55-line boundary
- Scene panel sidebar shows all scenes in order; updates live; clicking navigates editor to that scene
- Remote cursors with name labels and revision colors visible for all connected collaborators
- Presence avatars bar in editor toolbar shows all connected users
- Dogfood milestone (Week 8): team uses editor for real script writing; feedback logged
Parent: Script Service & Collaborative Editor

Title: As a writer I can write and edit scripts in a TipTap editor with professional industry-standard screenplay formatting
Description: Screenplay formatting is a professional standard that casting directors, producers, and line producers depend on. The editor must reproduce the visual and structural conventions of industry-standard tools (Final Draft, WriterDuet). The TipTap schema enforces element types; keyboard shortcuts must match writers' existing muscle memory. The visual output must be indistinguishable from a Final Draft export at a glance. Reference: WGA screenplay format spec, Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md TipTap Schema.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Editor renders in a page-like white container: Courier New 12pt, 1.5in left margin, 1in right margin, 1in top/bottom padding
- Scene Heading: UPPERCASE, full width, margin-top: 1em
- Action: mixed case, full width, no indent
- Character: UPPERCASE, left margin at approximately 3.7in (220px at 96dpi from left edge of content area)
- Dialogue: centred block, 2.5in wide, starting at approximately 2.5in from left edge of content area
- Parenthetical: inside dialogue indentation, text in (parentheses)
- Transition: right-flush, UPPERCASE (e.g. CUT TO:)
- Page break indicator shown as a dashed line at every 55-line boundary (CSS counter, not a real DOM break)
- Tab key cycles element types per WGA spec; empty nodes change type immediately; non-empty nodes do not change on Tab
- Enter after a Character node creates a Dialogue node; Enter in an empty Dialogue node creates a Character node
- All formatting implemented via TipTap node extensions and Tailwind/CSS classes; zero inline styles in the DOM
Parent: Screenplay Editor UI

Title: Create TipTap editor scaffold with 7 screenplay element node extensions and screenplay document schema
Description: Create apps/web/src/editor/ directory. Install: @tiptap/react @tiptap/pm @tiptap/starter-kit @tiptap/extension-collaboration @tiptap/extension-collaboration-cursor. Remove StarterKit paragraph, heading, and hardBreak defaults. Create apps/web/src/editor/extensions/nodes/: SceneHeadingNode.ts, ActionNode.ts, CharacterNode.ts, DialogueNode.ts, ParentheticalNode.ts, TransitionNode.ts, GeneralNode.ts. Each is a TipTap Node extension with: name matching AST node_type slug, group: 'block', content: 'inline*', parseHTML returning the element, renderHTML returning the element with a class matching the node name. Create SceneNode.ts wrapping a scene container. Create ScriptRootNode.ts as the top-level Document replacement with content: 'scene+'. Create apps/web/src/editor/Editor.tsx rendering useEditor with all extensions registered. Render in EditorPage replacing the Sprint 2 placeholder. Vitest test: create editor with SceneHeading content 'INT. OFFICE - DAY'; serialize to HTML; assert class='scene-heading' and text is uppercase.
Parent: As a writer I can write and edit scripts in a TipTap editor with professional industry-standard screenplay formatting

Title: Implement LoroPMBinding class bridging Loro CRDT and ProseMirror transactions without infinite loops
Description: Create apps/web/src/editor/crdt/LoroPMBinding.ts. Class LoroPMBinding with constructor(loroDoc: LoroDoc, editor: Editor) and bind()/unbind() methods. bind() sets up two listeners with infinite-loop guards: (1) Editor transaction listener: editor.on('transaction', (tr) => { if (tr.docChanged && !this._applyingRemote) { this._applyingLocal = true; this._applyPMTransactionToLoro(tr); this._applyingLocal = false; } }). (2) Loro subscription: loroDoc.subscribe((event) => { if (!this._applyingLocal && event.by !== 'local') { this._applyingRemote = true; const tr = this._applyLoroEventToPM(event); editor.view.dispatch(tr); this._applyingRemote = false; } }). Implement _applyPMTransactionToLoro: iterate tr.steps; for ReplaceStep extract from/to positions and inserted content; map to LoroText.insert(pos, text) / LoroText.delete(pos, len) on the correct LoroText node (looked up by scene id from the node's attrs). Implement _applyLoroEventToPM: map Loro TextDiff events (insert/delete) to PM transactions. Create LoroPMBindingContext React context and useLoroPMBinding hook. Write 10-step round-trip test (from POC task 97 — production quality with error handling and edge cases).
Parent: As a writer I can write and edit scripts in a TipTap editor with professional industry-standard screenplay formatting

Title: Implement Tab key auto-formatting extension cycling screenplay element types per WGA spec
Description: Create apps/web/src/editor/extensions/ScreenplayKeymap.ts as a TipTap Extension. Define keydown handlers for Tab and Shift+Tab. Tab cycle order: scene_heading → action → character → dialogue → parenthetical → action (loop back). Shift+Tab reverse: action → parenthetical → dialogue → character → action → scene_heading. Implementation: (1) In Tab handler get currentNodeType via editor.state.selection.$anchor.parent.type.name. (2) If the current node is empty (content.size === 0): call editor.chain().setNode(nextType).run() — change element type. (3) If the current node has content: do not change the type (to avoid accidentally changing a typed line); instead move cursor to end of next element. Also implement context-sensitive Enter behaviour: after Character (charNode.isEmpty === false) Enter creates a Dialogue node; in an empty Dialogue Enter creates a Character node; Enter in any other non-empty node creates an Action node by default. Write Vitest keyboard simulation tests for all Tab transitions and both Enter scenarios using TipTap's test utilities.
Parent: As a writer I can write and edit scripts in a TipTap editor with professional industry-standard screenplay formatting

Title: Apply screenplay visual formatting CSS for Courier 12pt industry-standard margins and page break indicators
Description: Create apps/web/src/editor/styles/screenplay.css imported into Editor.tsx. Page container (.screenplay-page): width: 816px (8.5in at 96dpi); min-height: 1056px (11in); background: white; box-shadow: 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.12); padding: 96px 72px 96px 108px (1in top/bottom, 0.75in right, 1.125in left — accounting for the overall content area margins). Typography: font-family: 'Courier Prime', 'Courier New', Courier, monospace; font-size: 12pt; line-height: 1.0; color: #000. Load Courier Prime from Google Fonts (400 weight) as the preferred monospace face. Element classes: .scene-heading { text-transform: uppercase; margin-top: 1em; margin-bottom: 0; } .character { margin-left: 220px; text-transform: uppercase; } .dialogue { margin-left: 100px; margin-right: 100px; } .parenthetical { margin-left: 130px; margin-right: 130px; } .transition { text-align: right; text-transform: uppercase; } Page break indicators: use a CSS counter (counter(page-lines)) approach — insert a .page-break-indicator div via a TipTap decoration at every 55 lines (implement as a Plugin that scans the doc on each transaction and adds widget decorations at the correct positions). Add a Playwright visual regression snapshot test: load editor with 2 pages of content; take screenshot; compare against reference image.
Parent: As a writer I can write and edit scripts in a TipTap editor with professional industry-standard screenplay formatting

Title: As a writer I can see collaborators cursor positions in real time and navigate scenes via the scene panel sidebar
Description: The scene panel and collaborative presence indicators make the editor feel alive and reduce disorientation in a long script. The scene panel is a fixed left sidebar showing all scenes in order; clicking a scene scrolls the editor. Remote cursors show each collaborator's name and revision color inline in the text. The presence toolbar shows avatars for all currently connected writers. These features consume Hocuspocus awareness state and the Loro MovableTree (not Postgres queries) so they update in real time without network round-trips. Reference: Wiki/04-crdt-collaboration.md Awareness Protocol, Wiki/15-roadmap.md Sprint 3 frontend tasks.
Acceptance Criteria:
- Scene panel (fixed 240px left sidebar; collapsible via toggle button): lists all scenes in order with scene number badge and heading text (truncated at 30 chars)
- Scene list updates live as scenes are added, moved, or deleted in the editor (driven by Loro doc subscription — no polling)
- Clicking a scene scrolls the editor viewport to that scene with smooth scroll
- Active scene highlighted in the panel as the cursor moves into a scene (updated on editor selectionUpdate event)
- Remote cursors: each connected collaborator's cursor shown as a 2px coloured caret with a floating name label; moves within 100ms of remote user's keystrokes
- Revision color on cursor label matches the awareness state revision color from the WGA 13-color palette
- Presence avatars bar in editor toolbar: up to 8 avatar circles with initials + revision color ring; 9+ shown as +N more tooltip
- All presence data driven by Hocuspocus awareness (no separate API call to Postgres)
Parent: Screenplay Editor UI

Title: Render remote cursor positions with collaborator name labels and revision color from Hocuspocus awareness data
Description: Create apps/web/src/editor/components/RemoteCursors.tsx. Props: awareness: Awareness (from @tiptap/extension-collaboration-cursor or Hocuspocus provider). Logic: (1) useEffect subscribing to awareness.on('change') — re-render on each awareness update. (2) For each remote awareness entry (userId !== localUserId): get anchor position from entry.cursor.anchor; call editor.view.coordsAtPos(anchor) to get { left, top } pixel coordinates relative to the editor DOM. (3) Render: a positioned absolutely div with class cursor-caret (2px wide, line-height tall, background = revisionColor); above it a div with class cursor-label showing userName with revisionColor background. (4) Clean up: entries with missing cursor field (null) render no cursor. CSS animation: caret blinks at 530ms via CSS @keyframes. Performance: wrap in React.memo; debounce awareness change handler to 30fps. Create PresenceAvatars.tsx for the toolbar: map awareness entries to circular Avatar components (2-char initials, revisionColor ring). Vitest test: mock awareness with 2 remote users → verify 2 cursor elements rendered with correct colors → mock one disconnect → verify 1 cursor remains.
Parent: As a writer I can see collaborators cursor positions in real time and navigate scenes via the scene panel sidebar

Title: Build scene panel sidebar with live scene list from Loro MovableTree and scroll-to-scene navigation
Description: Create apps/web/src/editor/components/ScenePanel.tsx. Data source: extract scene list from the LoroDoc MovableTree — not a GraphQL query. Subscribe to loroDoc.subscribe() to receive doc change events; on each event re-extract the ordered scene list: traverse MovableTree root children where node_type === 'scene'; for each collect { sceneId, sceneNumber: node.metadata.sceneNumber, headingText: getHeadingText(doc, sceneId) }. getHeadingText: find child node where node_type === 'scene_heading' and return metadata.textContent or 'Untitled Scene'. Render: (1) CollapseButton (ChevronLeft icon; toggle stored in localStorage key scene-panel-collapsed). (2) Scrollable scene list: ScenePanelItem per scene — badge with scene number, heading text truncated at 30 chars via CSS text-overflow: ellipsis, active state highlight. (3) Click handler: editor.commands.focus(); editor.commands.scrollIntoView() with the scene node resolved by nodeId using editor.state.doc.descendants(). Active scene: listen to editor.on('selectionUpdate') to find which scene contains the cursor; update activeSceneId state. Vitest test: LoroDoc with 3 scenes → ScenePanel renders 3 items with correct heading text → mock selection in scene 2 → assert scene 2 has active class.
Parent: As a writer I can see collaborators cursor positions in real time and navigate scenes via the scene panel sidebar
